page: Amazon Web Services m5ad.2xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an AMD EPYC 7571 processor running at 2.2 GHz. The instance provides 8 vCPUs (4 physical cores with 2 threads per core) and 32.0 GB of DDR4 memory operating at 3 GHz, yielding a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. It includes 300 GB of local NVMe SSD instance storage and a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the m5ad.2xlarge sits primarily in the lower tiers for compute-heavy workloads. Single-core stress-ng and Geekbench scores fall into the bottom 10%, while multi-core performance is categorized as weak (bottom 25%). Memory bandwidth tests also show weak performance, with large-block read speeds at 27.21 GB/sec and write speeds at 14.55 GB/sec. However, the instance achieves average ratings in Passmark CPU encryption and overall Passmark memory scores, indicating stable baseline capabilities for cryptographic and standard memory operations.

Given the presence of local NVMe SSD storage and a balanced memory-to-vCPU ratio, this instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads that require temporary high-speed local storage without the premium of dedicated high-compute instances. It is suitable for general-purpose workloads, basic database operations, static web serving, and lightweight application hosting where peak CPU performance is not the primary constraint.
description: Amazon Web Services m5ad.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 8 vCPUs and 32.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, powered by an AMD EPYC 7571 processor at 2.2 GHz. It includes 300 GB of local NVMe SSD instance storage and a 2 Gbps baseline network speed. Benchmark data shows weak to poor single-core and multi-core CPU performance, though it maintains average scores in Passmark CPU encryption and memory tests. With its balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and local NVMe storage, this instance is suited for general-purpose applications, static web serving, and workloads requiring temporary local storage.
